question: What does the -? modifier do in a mapped type: { [K in keyof T]-?: T[K] }?

options.text: Removes the optional modifier (?), making all properties required
Adds the optional modifier, making all properties optional
options.explanation: The minus sign (-) before ? means removing the modifier. To add it, just use ? without the minus

options.text: Removes undefined from the value type
options.explanation: -? removes the syntactic optionality modifier, not the undefined type from the value. Although the result is similar, the mechanism is different

options.text: Deletes properties whose value is undefined
options.explanation: -? doesn't filter properties — it transforms all keys by removing their optionality

question: How does key remapping via as work in: [K in keyof T as `get${Capitalize<K & string>}`]?

options.text: Renames each key by adding a get prefix and capitalizing the first letter
Filters keys, keeping only those starting with get
options.explanation: The as clause transforms keys, it doesn't filter them. Filtering only happens when as resolves to never

options.text: Creates additional keys while preserving the originals
options.explanation: The as clause replaces keys, it doesn't add new ones. Original keys are not preserved in the resulting type

options.text: Adds a runtime get method for each property
options.explanation: Mapped types work exclusively at the type level. They don't create runtime code or methods

question: Why is it important to check T[K] extends unknown[] when creating DeepPartial?

options.text: Without the check, arrays will be unwrapped as objects with numeric keys (0?, 1?, length?, etc.)
Arrays don't support recursive types
options.explanation: Arrays do support recursive types. The problem is that Array extends object = true, and without the check, the mapped type processes the array as an object

options.text: TypeScript will throw a compile error when recursing into an array
options.explanation: There won't be a compile error — the array will simply lose its type and turn into an object with numeric keys

options.text: Arrays don't have keyof
options.explanation: Arrays do have keyof — it's a union of numeric indices and methods (push, pop, length, etc.). That's exactly why a mapped type would unwrap the array as an object

question: Which of the following mapped types are homomorphic (preserving modifiers from the source type)?

options.text: { [K in keyof T]: T[K] } — iterating via keyof T
{ readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] } — adding readonly
{ [K in 'a' | 'b']: string } — iterating over a literal union
options.explanation: This is not a homomorphic mapped type since iteration goes over an explicit union, not keyof T. Homomorphism requires a connection to the source type via keyof

options.text: { [K in keyof T as `get${K & string}`]: T[K] } — with key remapping
options.explanation: Key remapping via as breaks homomorphism. Modifiers from the source type (readonly, optional) are not carried over when using an as clause

question: What happens if the as expression resolves to never?

options.text: The key is excluded from the resulting type
The value at that key becomes never
options.explanation: When as resolves to never, the entire key is skipped, not just its value. This is the primary mechanism for filtering keys in mapped types

options.text: A compile error occurs
options.explanation: never in the as position is a standard TypeScript mechanism for filtering keys, not an error

options.text: The key remains with type unknown
options.explanation: never in the as position means complete exclusion of the key, not replacing the type with unknown
